text.title: Error Prevention — Six Strategies and Field Patterns
text.summary: Six prevention strategies in order of preference, with concrete implementation patterns per input type.

rule.statement: Apply Nielsen Heuristic #5: eliminate error-prone conditions or present a confirmation option before users commit to a consequential action.
rule.rationale: Recovery is always more expensive than prevention — it costs user effort, time, and trust. Prevention eliminates the error path entirely; recovery only mitigates its impact.

rule.statement: Distinguish slips (right intention, wrong action) from mistakes (wrong intention) — they require different prevention strategies.
rule.rationale: Slips are prevented by constraints and confirmation; mistakes are prevented by providing better information and guidance. Applying confirmation dialogs to slip-prone actions without addressing the underlying ambiguity only slows users down.

rule.statement: Constrain input to valid values only — use pickers, dropdowns, and bounded fields instead of free-text fields wherever the valid value set is finite.
rule.rationale: A constrained input cannot produce an invalid value; a free-text field always can. Constraints eliminate the error before it is possible to make it.

example.note: Date: use a date picker that only allows valid dates, not a text field where users type dates. Quantity: min 1, max 99 with +/- buttons, not a free numeric field. Country: dropdown of countries, not "enter country name".

rule.statement: Pre-populate fields with the most likely correct value whenever context (location, history, profile) makes it determinable.
rule.rationale: Smart defaults reduce the number of fields users must fill in and simultaneously prevent errors on those fields by providing a correct starting value that requires no change for the majority of users.

example.note: Detect user location and pre-fill country. Pre-fill today's date for current events. Remember previous selections from the user's history.

rule.statement: Validate on blur for most fields; validate on keypress only for password strength and character counters. Never validate on keypress for format errors — wait until the user has finished typing.
rule.rationale: Validating format errors on every keystroke (showing "invalid email" when the user has typed "us") creates false error states that frustrate before any error has actually been made.

example.note: Password field: show requirements checklist, check each as user types (8+ characters, one uppercase, one number). Email field: validate format on blur — not while typing.

rule.statement: Use confirmation dialogs only for irreversible actions with significant consequences. Do not use them for routine, frequent, or easily reversible operations.
rule.rationale: Overuse of confirmations trains users to dismiss them without reading. This is worse than no confirmation — users click through the dialog reflexively and still trigger the unwanted action.

list.item: Use: permanent delete, cancel subscription, bulk destructive operations.
Do not use: save file, move to trash, log out, send message with undo available.

example.note: Good confirmation: "Delete 47 items permanently? This action cannot be undone. All selected files will be permanently removed." Buttons: [Cancel] [Delete 47 Items]. The button label repeats the count — users see the magnitude of what they are confirming.

rule.statement: Show expected format inside or below the field before the user starts typing; auto-format as the user types for structured data like phone numbers and credit cards.
rule.rationale: Users should not have to guess the format. Format shown after an error is too late — it should be visible before input begins.

section.title: Prevention by Field Type
section.p: Text: character limits with counter, format hints, auto-complete suggestions, real-time validation on blur.
Numeric: numeric keyboard on mobile, min/max constraints, increment buttons, format guidance for currency.
Date: date picker (never text input), disable invalid dates, clear format display, relative dates (Today, Tomorrow).
Selection: default selection when appropriate, logical ordering, search for lists over 10 items.
section.reference: Audit table for evaluating form fields and destructive actions
